summary: DHH reflects on how returning to in-person social interactions after Denmark's pandemic restrictions revealed the profound contrast between face-to-face conversation and social media discourse. He describes the subtle 'conversational ballet' of facial expressions, posture, and social graces that naturally moderates disagreement in person — something entirely absent online. He argues that pandemic isolation functioned as a form of microdosed torture that drove people to self-medicate with social media, creating a feedback loop of tribalism and anger. The antidote, he concludes, is simply spending more time with real people in person.
key_points: Face-to-face interaction is deeply nourishing even for introverts, and the contrast with online discourse became vivid after prolonged deprivation
In-person conversations have a natural 'ballet of social graces' — facial expressions, posture, pauses — that effortlessly moderates disagreement in ways social media cannot
Pandemic isolation created a feedback loop: loneliness leads to alienation, desperate virtual bonds, tribalism, and anger
Social media grew angrier during the pandemic not just because the world was harder, but because people lost the ability to process challenges together in person
DHH acknowledges the irony of a remote-work advocate arguing for the irreplaceable value of in-person human connection
money_quotes: The first revelation was that no matter how introvert you are, spending time face-to-face with others after prolonged periods of isolation is nourishing in a profound and human way (even if it's also exhausting).

The second revelation was just how normal and calm all these interactions and conversations were. No matter the topic. In stark contrast to what appears to be a continued deterioration of social media's ability to do the same.
Like if you cut out sugar for a month, then take your first bite of a frosted cake, the experience is vivid and detailed.
If everyone in your life has been reduced to an avatar for too long, the world does become very flat and two dimensional.
Where loneliness leads to alienation leads to despair leads to forming desperate virtual bonds leads to tribalism, hate, and a miserable quest for meaning.
There's a reason subjecting prisoners to total isolation for even a few days is considered torture. Even the most introverted among us need human connection. We're hardwired for it.
I think we may well look back upon these social dark ages of the pandemic as a grand social experiment of microdosing a huge swath of the population with this unique form of isolation torture.
But the antidote is there. It's waiting. It's other people, in flesh and blood, without a mask, without an agenda.
key_insight: The pandemic revealed that social media's growing toxicity is less about the platforms themselves and more about the isolation that drives people to them — and the cure is simply being with other humans in person.
